questions.questionText: The classic presentation of Nephrotic Syndrome includes all EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: Hematuria is characteristic of Nephritic syndrome, not Nephrotic syndrome.

questions.questionText: Red blood cell casts in the urine are highly indicative of:
questions.explanation: RBC casts mean the RBCs were molded in the renal tubules, a sign of glomerular bleeding.

questions.questionText: The most common cause of Nephrotic syndrome in children is:
questions.explanation: MCD responds well to steroids and shows only effacement of podocyte foot processes on EM.

questions.questionText: A subepithelial 'lumpy-bumpy' deposit pattern on electron microscopy is typical for:
questions.explanation: Caused by subepithelial immune complex deposition 2-4 weeks after a strep infection.

questions.questionText: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules in the glomerulus are virtually pathognomonic for:
questions.explanation: These nodular glomerulosclerotic lesions are classic for diabetic kidney disease.

questions.questionText: Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN) is most commonly caused by:
questions.explanation: ATN is the most common cause of intrinsic acute kidney injury.

questions.questionText: The presence of 'muddy brown' granular casts in the urine is highly suggestive of:
questions.explanation: The sloughing of necrotic tubular epithelial cells forms these casts.

questions.questionText: White blood cell casts in the urine are a hallmark of:
questions.explanation: WBC casts indicate inflammation in the kidney itself, differentiating pyelonephritis from lower UTI.

questions.questionText: The most common type of renal stone is composed of:
questions.explanation: Calcium stones (mostly oxalate) account for ~80% of kidney stones.

questions.questionText: Clear cell Renal Cell Carcinoma is often associated with the loss of which tumor suppressor gene?
questions.explanation: VHL mutation leads to increased HIF-1 expression and highly vascular tumors.

questions.questionText: In Renal Pathology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Pathology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Pathology references for Renal Pathology. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Renal Pathology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Renal Pathology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Renal Pathology.
